title: The Ensure Block 🛡️
description: Guarantee that crucial cleanup code runs, crash or no crash.

slides.title: Closing Time 🧹
slides.content:
Imagine your program is downloading a massive secure file.

Suddenly, the internet cuts out. An Exception is thrown! Your program instantly crashes and dies midway through the download.

The secure file is now stuck permanently open, draining your computer's memory. This is called a memory leak.

slides.title: The `ensure` Keyword
slides.content:
To absolutely guarantee that the file gets closed, regardless of whether a crash occurred, you place cleanup code inside an `ensure` block.

The `ensure` block flawlessly guarantees that its code will be executed exactly right before the program terminates or finishes.

slides.title: Ensuring Execution
slides.content: Run this! Even though the array division crashes the program instantly without a rescue block, Ruby strictly pauses its own death to run our `ensure` block first!
slides.codeSnippet:
begin
  puts "Opening file..."
  crash_the_app = [1, 2, 3] / 5
ensure
  puts "Closing the file safely! The leak is prevented!"
end

quizData.question: Which keyword acts as the absolute final safeguard, guaranteeing its code will flawlessly run even if a fatal unhandled Exception instantly destroys the block above it?
quizData.explanation: If the `begin` block generates an Exception, Ruby pauses, executes the `ensure` block, and then finally dies.

slides.title: Putting it all Together 🧩
slides.content:
The ultimate defensive structure handles everything gracefully:

```ruby
begin
  # 1. Do risky stuff
rescue ErrorClass => e
  # 2. Fix the specific error gracefully
ensure
  # 3. Always clean up the messy data here after 1 or 2 finishes
end
```

quizData.question: If your `begin` block runs perfectly flawlessly with zero exceptions at all, does the `ensure` block still run?

options.text: No, it only runs on errors.

quizData.explanation: `ensure` ALWAYS runs. That's its entire purpose. It is the final guaranteed step of the block execution.
